spec_match.rationale: Page describes the cross-origin-iframe motivating use case clearly and includes an iframe + parent setPreferredSinkId call. But the iframe is same-origin (acknowledged in the warn block), so the demo cannot actually prove the cross-origin claim.

interactive.rationale: Real getUserMedia + enumerate + setPreferredSinkId + iframe reload via postMessage — interactive enough. The iframe itself (./player.html) is referenced but its behaviour can't be verified from this page alone.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb sells 'cross-origin iframe routing — without postMessage' but the iframe is same-origin and the parent IS using postMessage to tell it to reload. The framing oversells what's verifiable here.

edge_cases.rationale: Honestly notes the same-origin limitation in the warn block. Doesn't show what happens if the iframe is sandboxed without allow-same-origin, if the parent's permissions policy denies speaker selection, or if a deeper nested iframe is reached.

references.rationale: Links to chromestatus.com/feature/5098834847072256 and the W3C mediacapture-output spec section.

design_system.rationale: Variables throughout, warn-block uses --accent-rose, iframe is dashed-bordered with --border-black.

openQuestions.title: Host the player on a real cross-origin frame
openQuestions.detail: The whole concept needs a genuinely different origin to prove the API actually crosses the boundary. Either serve player.html from a different deno-deploy subdomain, embed a permissive demo origin (e.g. https://example.com style mock), or wire server.ts to mount an /audio-iframe path that the page treats as cross-origin. Without that, the demo can't deliver on its headline claim.

openQuestions.title: Show the permissions-policy gate
openQuestions.detail: setPreferredSinkId is gated by Permissions Policy 'speaker-selection'. Add a second iframe with allow-attribute set vs missing to demonstrate the gate — currently invisible.

openQuestions.title: Stop using postMessage in the after path
openQuestions.detail: The 'no postMessage handshake' claim is undermined by the parent calling frame.contentWindow.postMessage({kind:'reload'}). That message exists to force the iframe to re-create its audio element so it picks up the new preference, but it reads exactly like the postMessage handshake the page is selling against. Either rewrite the iframe to listen via a different mechanism (e.g. devicechange event in the iframe) or call out in prose that the reload is purely a demo convenience.

summary: Communicates the motivating use case well in prose, but the implementation can't deliver on it — the iframe is same-origin and the parent still postMessages it to reload. To stand up to the claim, the demo needs a genuinely cross-origin iframe (server-side change) and a quieter way for the iframe to react to the new preferred sink.
